title: OSI Layer Model – 2 & 5 Marks Only (Total 100 Marks)

questions.q: How many layers are in the OSI model?
questions.answer: Seven layers.

questions.q: What is the full form of OSI?
questions.answer: Open Systems Interconnection.

questions.q: Which layer is responsible for routing packets?
questions.answer: Network Layer (Layer 3).

questions.q: What is the PDU at the Data Link Layer?
questions.answer: Frame.

questions.q: Which device operates at the Physical Layer?
questions.answer: Hub or repeater.

questions.q: What is the PDU at the Transport Layer when using TCP?
questions.answer: Segment.

questions.q: Name any two protocols that work at the Application Layer.
questions.answer: HTTP and FTP (or SMTP, DNS, Telnet).

questions.q: What does MAC address stand for and at which layer is it used?
questions.answer: Media Access Control address; used at Data Link Layer (Layer 2).

questions.q: Which layer handles data compression and encryption?
questions.answer: Presentation Layer (Layer 6).

questions.q: What is the purpose of a checksum in the Transport Layer?
questions.answer: Error detection – to check if the segment is corrupted during transmission.

questions.q: Which layer is responsible for establishing, maintaining, and terminating sessions?
questions.answer: Session Layer (Layer 5).

questions.q: What is a Service Access Point (SAP)? Give one example.
questions.answer: A point where a layer provides services to the layer above. Example: port numbers (e.g., TCP port 80).

questions.q: Which OSI layer does a router operate at?
questions.answer: Network Layer (Layer 3).

questions.q: What does the acronym PDU stand for?
questions.answer: Protocol Data Unit.

questions.q: What is the difference between a segment and a datagram?
questions.answer: Segment is the Transport Layer PDU for TCP (connection-oriented); datagram is the PDU for UDP (connectionless).

questions.q: Which layer ensures node-to-node delivery?
questions.answer: Data Link Layer (Layer 2).

questions.q: What is the role of the trailer in a Data Link frame?
questions.answer: It contains the Frame Check Sequence (e.g., CRC) for error detection.

questions.q: Name one protocol that works at the Network Layer.
questions.answer: IP (Internet Protocol).

questions.q: What is 'encapsulation' in the OSI model?
questions.answer: The process where each layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer) to the data received from the layer above.

questions.q: Which layer provides logical addressing (IP addresses)?
questions.answer: Network Layer (Layer 3).

questions.q: Explain the responsibilities of the Physical Layer and the Data Link Layer in the OSI model. Include examples of devices and protocols for each.
questions.answer: Physical Layer (Layer 1): Transmits raw bits over a physical medium. Defines voltage levels, data rates, connectors (RJ45), and synchronization. Devices: hubs, repeaters. Protocols: Ethernet (physical part), DSL. Data Link Layer (Layer 2): Provides node-to-node reliable delivery. Packages bits into frames, adds MAC addresses, performs error detection (CRC) and flow control. Devices: switches, bridges. Protocols: Ethernet (MAC sublayer), PPP, Wi-Fi (802.11 MAC).

questions.q: Describe the difference between connection-oriented and connectionless services at the Transport Layer. Give one example protocol for each and a typical use case.
questions.answer: Connection-oriented (TCP): Requires connection setup (three-way handshake). Provides reliability, sequencing, flow control. Example: Web browsing (HTTP). Connectionless (UDP): No connection setup, no guarantees. Low overhead. Example: VoIP, video streaming, DNS queries.

questions.q: Explain the role of the Presentation Layer. Give three specific functions with examples.
questions.answer: Functions: (1) Data translation – ASCII to EBCDIC conversion. (2) Data compression – JPEG, MPEG. (3) Data encryption/decryption – SSL/TLS (conceptually).

questions.q: What is the difference between a switch and a hub? Relate to OSI layers and performance.
questions.answer: Hub: Physical Layer – repeats signals to all ports, causes collisions, shared bandwidth. Switch: Data Link Layer – forwards frames based on MAC address, creates separate collision domains, improves performance.

questions.q: Explain the concept of encapsulation and decapsulation with a step-by-step example of sending a web request.
questions.answer: Encapsulation (sender): Application (HTTP data) -> Transport (TCP segment) -> Network (IP packet) -> Data Link (Ethernet frame) -> Physical (bits). Decapsulation (receiver): reverse process – each layer removes its header and passes data up.

questions.q: What are the functions of the Session Layer? Explain checkpointing with an example.
questions.answer: Session Layer establishes, manages, and terminates sessions. Checkpointing: inserts checkpoints in data stream. If failure occurs, transfer resumes from last checkpoint instead of restarting. Example: large file download – after 600MB of 1GB, resume from 600MB.

questions.q: Compare the OSI model with the TCP/IP model. Mention number of layers and key differences.
questions.answer: OSI: 7 layers (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application). TCP/IP: 4 layers (Network Interface, Internet, Transport, Application). OSI separates Session/Presentation; TCP/IP merges them into Application. OSI is theoretical; TCP/IP is practical based on ARPANET.

questions.q: What is a Protocol Data Unit (PDU)? Name the PDU for each of the seven OSI layers.
questions.answer: PDU is data exchanged between peer layers. Layer 7: Data; Layer 6: Data; Layer 5: Data; Layer 4: Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP); Layer 3: Packet; Layer 2: Frame; Layer 1: Bits.

questions.q: Explain how multiplexing and demultiplexing work at the Transport Layer using port numbers.
questions.answer: Multiplexing: multiple applications send data via the same IP address; each gets a unique source port. Transport layer collects and passes to network. Demultiplexing at receiver: transport layer reads destination port and delivers data to correct application.

questions.q: Describe the two sublayers of the Data Link Layer (LLC and MAC) and their functions.
questions.answer: LLC (Logical Link Control): provides multiplexing/demultiplexing using SAPs (EtherType), flow control, error notification. MAC (Media Access Control): controls access to physical medium (CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA), adds MAC addresses, generates CRC for error detection.

questions.q: A user can ping a remote server by IP address but cannot browse the website hosted on it. Which OSI layers could be the problem and why?
questions.answer: Possible layers: Transport Layer – web uses TCP port 80/443, maybe blocked by firewall; Presentation Layer – TLS certificate issue for HTTPS; Application Layer – web server not running or DNS issue (if domain not resolved). Ping works, so Physical, Data Link, Network are fine.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the checksum and sequence numbers in the TCP header?
questions.answer: Checksum: error detection – verifies segment integrity. Sequence numbers: (1) ensure ordered delivery (reassembly), (2) detect duplicate segments, (3) enable acknowledgments and retransmission of lost data.
